content.text: CoE Operating Cadence and Intake Process

A Claude Center of Excellence (CoE) is only as effective as its day-to-day operating rhythm. Without a deliberate cadence, important activities slip — intake requests go unacknowledged, metrics go unmeasured, and stakeholders lose confidence. This document defines the operating cadence structure for a Claude CoE and the intake process that makes it reliable.

Operating cadence refers to the recurring meetings, reviews, and communication rhythms that keep the CoE functioning. Intake process refers to how teams submit requests and how the CoE receives, triages, and fulfills them. Both must work together for the CoE to deliver consistent value.

Without a defined cadence, CoE activity becomes reactive and uneven. Some weeks, the team is overwhelmed with ad-hoc requests; other weeks, important governance or enablement work gets deferred indefinitely. A structured cadence solves this by:
teams know when to expect updates, when to submit requests, and when steering committee reviews occur.
recurring slots for roadmap, metrics, and policy reviews prevent operational noise from crowding out higher-order work.
recurring check-ins surface SLA misses, blocked requests, and capacity issues before they become crises.
monthly and quarterly reporting makes CoE value visible to sponsors who don't see day-to-day activity.
Cadence is the CoE's metabolism. Too slow and the organization loses confidence; too frantic and the team burns out. Calibrate it to your size and adoption stage.
A mature Claude CoE operates on four time horizons simultaneously:

attrs.alt: CoE operating cadence — four-layer rhythm model

content.text: Weekly activities keep the intake pipeline moving and SLAs intact. These should be lightweight and focused — the weekly cadence is about operational hygiene, not strategic discussion.
CoE team syncs on active requests, blockers, upcoming deliveries
Review new requests submitted since last triage; assign owners
Check all open tickets for SLA compliance; escalate at-risk ones
Brief async update to stakeholders on CoE activity (Slack or email)
Many small CoEs (2–3 FTE) run weekly standup and intake triage in a single 45-minute block. Larger teams may separate them to keep each meeting focused.
Monthly activities build the stakeholder relationship and surface trends that aren't visible week-to-week:
Metrics summary: request volume, SLA compliance, CSAT, capacity
CoE-facilitated session on a Claude topic; open to all internal teams
Internal CoE review of operations report before distribution
Document new use cases discovered during the month; add to repository
Brief executive sponsors on CoE progress, wins, and risks
The monthly operations report is the most important artifact for CoE visibility. It should be concise — one page or a short slide — and focused on trends, not individual tickets.
Quarterly reviews align the CoE with organizational priorities and drive continuous improvement:
present CoE performance to executive sponsors and governance representatives. Discuss roadmap, resource needs, and risks.
assess progress against quarterly objectives. Adjust goals if scope or priorities have shifted.
evaluate the service catalog for gaps and redundancy. Add services that emerging demand justifies; retire services with no uptake.
forecast demand for the next quarter and assess whether the CoE team is sized appropriately.
review Claude usage policies for needed updates driven by new capabilities, incidents, or regulatory changes.

# Sample quarterly steering committee agenda (60 min)

1. CoE performance dashboard review              [15 min]
   - Request volume and SLA trends (last 90 days)
   - CSAT scores and qualitative feedback
   - Capacity utilization

2. Use case portfolio update                      [10 min]
   - New use cases deployed in the quarter
   - Notable business outcomes

3. Roadmap review                                 [15 min]
   - Progress on quarterly initiatives
   - Proposed Q+1 priorities
   - Resource asks (headcount, tooling, budget)

4. Risks and open issues                          [10 min]
   - Policy gaps or compliance risks
   - Blocked initiatives

5. Open Q&A with steering committee              [10 min]
Annual activities set the multi-year direction and secure the resources needed to sustain CoE growth:
revisit the CoE charter and mission in light of organizational AI strategy evolution. Update the 3-year roadmap.
evaluate whether the CoE's composition (skills, roles, reporting structure) still fits the organization's needs.
prepare the next fiscal year's CoE budget, including headcount, training, tooling, and operational costs.
benchmark the CoE against an external maturity model (e.g., AI CoE maturity frameworks from Gartner or internal benchmarks). Identify the next maturity milestone.
annual presentation to senior leadership highlighting CoE impact, business value delivered, and strategic priorities for the coming year.

The intake process is how teams make requests and how the CoE receives, evaluates, and fulfills them. A well-designed intake process is fast, low-friction for requesters, and efficient for the CoE team.

Teams should be able to submit requests through a single, well-known channel. Avoid fragmented channels (email alias, Slack, ticketing system, AND a form) — they create duplicate requests and gaps in tracking.
team name, contact person, urgency level
dropdown from the service catalog (or 'I'm not sure' to trigger triage)
2–5 sentences on what the team is trying to accomplish with Claude
when does the team need the service delivered?
has the team already started something the CoE should be aware of?

# Example intake form fields (ServiceNow / Jira / Google Forms)
form:
  - field: team_name
    type: text
    required: true

  - field: contact_email
    type: email
    required: true

  - field: service_requested
    type: select
    options:
      - "Prompt Engineering Workshop"
      - "API Integration Review"
      - "Use Case Risk Assessment"
      - "Use Case Discovery Workshop"
      - "Model Selection Consultation"
      - "Prompt Library Access"
      - "Other (describe below)"
    required: true

  - field: use_case_description
    type: textarea
    placeholder: "What are you trying to accomplish with Claude?"
    required: true

  - field: urgency
    type: select
    options: ["Standard (SLA applies)", "Expedited (discuss with CoE)"]
    required: true

  - field: target_date
    type: date
    required: false

  - field: additional_context
    type: textarea
    required: false
After a request is submitted, the CoE runs a structured triage process:
send an automated or manual acknowledgment within the defined response SLA (typically 1–2 business days).
does it match a defined catalog service? If yes, proceed. If not, escalate to the CoE Lead to assess whether it's in-scope, out-of-scope, or reveals a catalog gap.
identify the CoE specialist responsible for fulfillment based on category (enablement, technical, governance, innovation).
calculate the delivery date from the intake date using the service's SLA. Communicate this to the requester.
log the request in the CoE's ticketing system with requester info, assigned owner, service, and target date.
owner begins fulfillment according to the service definition's standard procedure.
Teams will sometimes request things the CoE hasn't formally defined as services. A robust intake process handles this without dropping the request:
Log the request in a dedicated 'out-of-catalog' backlog for monthly review
Acknowledge the team and explain that their request will be reviewed within 5 business days
In the monthly metrics review, tally out-of-catalog requests to identify catalog gaps
If the CoE can fulfill the request informally (under 2 hours of effort), do so and track it
If the request is substantial and recurring, create a new catalog service entry and then fulfill it
If the request is clearly out of scope (e.g., general IT support), redirect to the appropriate team
Out-of-catalog requests are signals, not noise. They reveal where teams have unmet needs the CoE hasn't yet formalized — track them, analyze them, and act on them.
SLA Management Within the Intake Process
SLAs only work if the CoE actively monitors them. A request that silently misses its SLA destroys more trust than a request where the CoE proactively communicates a delay.
Define clear SLA escalation triggers so issues surface early:
Request not acknowledged within response SLA
Delivery date within 3 business days; no update sent
Owner sends proactive status update to requester
Delivery date passed; request still open
CoE Lead notified; requester contacted with new date
CoE Lead calls requester to clarify within 4 hours
When the CoE cannot meet an SLA, proactive communication is mandatory:

# SLA delay communication template

Subject: Update on your CoE request — [Service Name]

Hi [Team],

We're reaching out to update you on your request for [Service Name],
submitted on [Date].

Unfortunately, we're unable to deliver by the original target date of [Date].
The revised delivery date is [New Date].

Reason for delay: [brief, honest explanation — capacity, dependency, scope clarification]

We apologize for the inconvenience. Your request remains our priority and
the assigned owner is [Name] (reachable at [contact]).

If you have urgent questions, please reach out to [CoE Lead Name].

Thank you for your patience.
[CoE Team]
Like the service catalog, the intake process matures over time. Teams can assess where they sit and what to target next:
Requests via email / Slack DM; no tracking; no SLAs; triage happens ad-hoc
Intake form exists; manual triage; SLAs defined but not systematically tracked
Ticketing system; automatic acknowledgments; SLA tracking and escalations; monthly metrics
Catalog portal with auto-routing; teams track their own requests; CoE notified when review needed
Internal service portal with workflow automation
Most CoEs should target Level 3 within 12 months of formation. Level 4 is appropriate for CoEs processing 50+ requests per month where manual triage creates bottlenecks.
The right tooling depends on organizational context and request volume. Common options:
low-cost, quick to deploy, adequate for early-stage CoEs with <10 requests/month. Loses scalability fast.
similar to above; better if the organization runs on Microsoft 365.
strong SLA tracking, escalation rules, and reporting. Good for technical organizations already using Jira.
enterprise-grade ITSM; appropriate for large organizations where the CoE is part of a broader IT service catalog. High setup cost.
Notion/Confluence as the catalog browsing front-end, Jira for ticket tracking. Flexible and lightweight.
Choose tooling that integrates with what the organization already uses. A CoE intake form that requires a new login or tool adoption creates unnecessary friction.
The cadence model and intake process are not static. Each cadence layer feeds information into improvement cycles:
immediate feedback on request quality, common confusion points in the intake form, and recurring questions that reveal documentation gaps.
SLA trends reveal capacity problems or service definitions that are unclear. CSAT scores surface quality issues. Lunch-and-learn attendance signals topic interest.
patterns across months reveal systematic gaps in the service catalog, structural capacity issues, or misalignment between CoE priorities and organizational needs.
a full maturity assessment identifies the next capability investment — whether that's headcount, tooling, new service categories, or expanded stakeholder relationships.
The cadence model is how the CoE learns. Each loop of weekly → monthly → quarterly → annual review is a structured feedback cycle that makes the CoE incrementally more effective.
Even well-intentioned CoEs fall into predictable operating cadence traps:
CoE team spends more time in meetings than doing work
Cap meetings to 20% of CoE capacity; replace status meetings with async updates
Add automatic escalation alerts; review SLA misses in weekly standup
Teams bypass form and email directly
Trim intake form to 5–6 fields; gather remaining details in kickoff call
Shorten report to one page; lead with business impact, not activity metrics
Quarterly review becomes a status dump
Structure quarterly as a decision-making session, not a report-out
A well-defined operating cadence and intake process transforms a Claude CoE from an ad-hoc support function into a reliable, scalable organizational capability.
Structure cadence across four layers: weekly (operational), monthly (stakeholder), quarterly (strategic), annual (long-horizon)
Weekly intake triage and SLA monitoring prevent request backlogs and keep commitments visible
Monthly operations reports make CoE value tangible for sponsors who don't see daily activity
Quarterly steering committee reviews maintain strategic alignment and unlock resources
Design the intake process for low requester friction: a single channel, a short form, and fast acknowledgment
Actively manage SLAs with escalation triggers and proactive delay communication
Use out-of-catalog requests as signals for catalog gaps — track, analyze, and act on them monthly
Mature the intake process incrementally: start with a form, graduate to a ticketing system as volume grows
